title: Pearl's Ladder as an Operator Hierarchy
subtitle: Why confusing correlation with causation is literally a type error — and how three classes of operators formalize the Causal Hierarchy

children.children: Judea Pearl's Ladder of Causation is one of the most important ideas in modern AI. It says there are three fundamentally different kinds of reasoning — and confusing them is not just a mistake, it's a category error.

What makes this relevant to Schankian operators? Each rung corresponds to a different CLASS of operator with different structural constraints. Rung Collapse — substituting a lower-rung operator where a higher one is needed — is literally a type error in operator algebra.

items.definition: Observing correlations in data. 'Patients who take Drug X recover more often.' Operator: conditioning P(Y|X). No intervention, no manipulation — just watching patterns.

Actively manipulating the world. 'What happens IF I give Drug X?' Operator: do-calculus P(Y|do(X)). Requires graph surgery — cutting incoming edges to X. Fundamentally different from just observing X.

Reasoning about alternative histories. 'Would the patient have recovered WITHOUT Drug X?' Operator: abduction → action → prediction with fixed exogenous variables. Requires the full structural causal model.

children.children: The Causal Hierarchy Theorem (Bareinboim et al., 2022) proves something remarkable: each rung is STRICTLY more powerful than the one below. No amount of Rung 1 data can answer Rung 2 questions. No amount of Rung 2 experiments can answer Rung 3 counterfactuals.

left.title: Programming Type System
items: Passing int where string expected = type error
Each type has its own valid operations
No implicit casting between incompatible types

right.title: Causal Type System
items: Using correlation where causation needed = rung collapse
A causal type checker would catch this
Each rung has its own valid inferences

children.bridgeText: Rung Collapse is the causal equivalent of a type error — using a value at the wrong level of abstraction.

children.children: Operator algebras nest strictly: A₁ ⊊ A₂ ⊊ A₃. Each higher rung CONTAINS the lower rung's operators but adds new ones that cannot be reduced. This is not a matter of having more data — it's a mathematical impossibility.
Let's formalize each rung as a class of Schankian operator with specific structural constraints.

L1 operators compute P(Y|X) — they update beliefs based on observations. In Schankian terms, these are OBSERVE operators: seeing X changes our predictions about Y. They work entirely within the joint distribution. No causal structure needed.
Example: Seeing wet grass (X) makes us predict rain (Y). But this is symmetric — P(wet|rain) and P(rain|wet) both follow from the joint. L1 can't tell us which caused which.

L2 operators compute P(Y|do(X)) — they predict the effects of ACTIONS. The key operation is 'graph surgery': when you DO something, you sever all incoming causal arrows to X. You're not observing X — you're FORCING it.
This is where Schankian operators shine. ATRANS, PROPEL, MTRANS are all interventions — they change the world. 'John gave Mary the book' is do(ATRANS(book, John→Mary)), not an observation.

L3 operators answer 'what would have happened if...?' via three steps: (1) ABDUCTION: use evidence to infer exogenous variables U, (2) ACTION: modify the structural equations, (3) PREDICTION: compute the outcome in the modified model with the SAME U values.

This is operator INVERSION — running the causal model backwards, then forwards on a different path. It requires the full structural causal model, not just the graph. It's the most powerful and most demanding level.
Real-world reasoning constantly confuses rungs. Can you identify which rung each claim belongs to — and spot where rung collapse occurs?

props.title: Classify the Reasoning Rung

scenarios.claim: Countries that eat more chocolate win more Nobel Prizes.
scenarios.explanation: Pure correlation from observational data. No intervention — we're just seeing that chocolate consumption and Nobel Prizes co-occur. Confounding variable: wealth enables both chocolate and research funding.

scenarios.claim: If we give everyone in this country chocolate, they'll win more Nobel Prizes.
scenarios.explanation: RUNG COLLAPSE! This treats a Rung 1 correlation as if it were a Rung 2 intervention. do(chocolate) ≠ observe(chocolate). The correlation disappears under intervention because the confound (wealth) is not affected by chocolate distribution.

scenarios.claim: Randomized trial: Drug X reduces symptoms by 30% vs placebo.
scenarios.explanation: A proper Rung 2 claim. Randomization = graph surgery (severs confounders). We're measuring P(recovery | do(Drug X)), not just P(recovery | took Drug X).

scenarios.claim: This patient took Drug X and recovered. Would they have recovered without it?
scenarios.explanation: Rung 3 counterfactual about a SPECIFIC individual. Requires knowing this patient's exogenous variables U (genetics, lifestyle, disease severity) and re-running the causal model without Drug X.

scenarios.claim: Students who attend tutoring get higher grades, so tutoring causes better grades.
scenarios.explanation: RUNG COLLAPSE! The observation (Rung 1) is that tutored students score higher. But motivated students self-select into tutoring — the causal arrow might run from motivation → both tutoring AND grades. Claiming causation requires a Rung 2 intervention (randomly assigning tutoring).

props.description: For each claim, identify which rung of Pearl's Ladder it belongs to. Watch for Rung Collapse — where a lower-rung observation is incorrectly treated as a higher-rung conclusion.

props.title: Category Theory: Why the Mappings Fail

children.children: Category theory gives us a precise language for WHY rung collapse fails. If each rung is a category (objects = variables, morphisms = operator applications), then the inter-rung mappings are NOT functors.

A functor preserves structure — if A→B→C in one category, the mapped version must satisfy F(A)→F(B)→F(C). But going from Rung 2 down to Rung 1, causal chains get destroyed. do(X)→Y doesn't map to any consistent observation X→Y because confounders break the chain.

The inter-rung mappings are information-destroying — they're forgetful functors at best, and at worst not even functors. This is the mathematical content of 'correlation is not causation': the map from L2 to L1 forgets the graph structure that makes causal claims meaningful.
In Simpson's Paradox, a correlation reverses when you condition on a confounder. Drug helps overall, but hurts in every subgroup (or vice versa).

Category-theoretically: the Rung 1 morphism (correlation) and the Rung 2 morphism (causal effect) point in OPPOSITE directions. The mapping between rungs doesn't just lose information — it can REVERSE the arrow. No functor can do this.
Here's the punchline for Schankian operators: if we type-annotate our operators with their causal rung, we get a compile-time check against rung collapse.
Each Schankian operator gets a rung annotation:

• OBSERVE_correlation : L1 — 'I see X and Y co-occur'
• ATRANS, PROPEL, MTRANS : L2 — 'doing X causes Y to change'
• WOULD_HAVE(op, ¬condition) : L3 — 'Y would not have changed without X'

A type checker ensures: if a reasoning chain requires L2 operators, you can't substitute L1 observations. If counterfactual reasoning requires L3, you need the full structural model — not just interventional data.

props.title: Type-Check the Operator Chain

chains.description: Observing smoke → concluding fire → planning escape route
chains.explanation: The jump from observing to planning requires L2 — you need to know that do(escape_route) → safety. Pure observation can't justify action planning.

chains.description: Running RCT → measuring drug effect → recommending treatment
chains.explanation: Valid chain: the RCT provides L2 evidence, measurement drops to L1 (which is fine — higher rungs contain lower ones), and recommendation is justified at L2.

chains.description: Patient died → would they have lived with treatment? → was doctor negligent?
chains.explanation: Valid typing: legal negligence genuinely requires L3 counterfactual reasoning. You need to imagine the specific alternative world where this specific patient got treatment.

props.description: Each reasoning chain uses operators at different rungs. Check whether the types are consistent — does each step have the causal information it needs?

children.children: Sridhar Mahadevan's Universal Causality framework recasts ALL of causal inference as lifting problems in category theory. A causal question is: given a diagram of known relationships, can you 'lift' to find the unknown causal effect?
This connects beautifully to our operator framework. Each rung provides different lifting capabilities:

items.definition: Can lift observations to predictions about other observations. Limited to the joint distribution. Like navigating a flat map — you can go anywhere on the plane but can't reach higher floors.
Can lift interventions to predictions about effects. Requires the causal DAG. Like having an elevator — you can reach floors the flat map can't, but only floors in THIS building.

Can lift to counterfactual worlds. Requires the full structural equations with exogenous variables. Like having a portal to parallel buildings — you can reason about what would have happened in alternative histories.

children.children: Large language models are trained on Rung 1 data — text correlations. Yet they're asked to answer Rung 2 and Rung 3 questions constantly. 'What would happen if...?' 'Did X cause Y?' 'Would Z have prevented the outcome?'

LLMs perform Rung Collapse by default — they approximate causal and counterfactual reasoning with sophisticated pattern matching on text. Sometimes this works (because text encodes causal knowledge implicitly), but it fails systematically on novel causal structures.

Schankian operators, typed by causal rung, could provide the missing structure. Instead of asking an LLM to pattern-match its way to a causal conclusion, we'd have it: (1) classify the question's rung, (2) select operators of the appropriate type, (3) reject the inference if the available evidence is below the required rung.

Sycophancy is a form of Rung Collapse: the model replaces causal reasoning (L2/L3) with social pattern matching (L1). 'The user seems to want answer X' is an L1 observation. 'Answer X is correct because of Y' requires L2 causal structure. Sycophantic models collapse from L2 to L1 under social pressure.

questions.question: A study finds that people who carry lighters are more likely to get lung cancer. What rung does this claim live on?
questions.explanation: This is pure Rung 1 — an observed correlation. The confounder is smoking: smokers carry lighters AND get cancer. Distributing lighters (Rung 2 intervention) wouldn't cause cancer.

questions.question: The Causal Hierarchy Theorem proves that:
options: More data at Rung 1 eventually answers Rung 2 questions
Each rung is strictly more powerful than those below — no amount of lower-rung data suffices
Counterfactuals are just very precise interventions
Randomized controlled trials answer all causal questions
questions.explanation: The theorem proves STRICT separation. You cannot derive interventional conclusions from observational data alone, nor counterfactual conclusions from interventional data alone, regardless of sample size.

questions.question: In category theory terms, why does Rung Collapse fail?
options: The inter-rung mappings are not structure-preserving functors
questions.explanation: The mappings between rungs DESTROY information (causal structure). A functor must preserve composition, but collapsing from L2 to L1 can reverse causal arrows (Simpson's Paradox). The mapping is information-destroying, not functorial.

questions.question: How does Pearl's Ladder connect to Schankian operators?
options: Each rung defines a different class of operator with different structural constraints
questions.explanation: Operators are typed by rung: OBSERVE operators are L1, ATRANS/PROPEL/etc. are L2 (interventions), and counterfactual operators (WOULD_HAVE) are L3. The type system prevents rung collapse by construction.

questions.question: An LLM trained only on text is fundamentally operating at which rung?
options: Rung 2 — because it models cause and effect in narratives
Rung 3 — because it can answer 'what if' questions
All three — because text contains causal information
questions.explanation: LLMs are trained on observational text data (Rung 1). They can APPROXIMATE higher-rung reasoning because text implicitly encodes causal knowledge, but they lack the structural guarantees. They perform Rung Collapse — sophisticated pattern matching masquerading as causal reasoning.

children.children: Pearl's Ladder gives us a principled framework for understanding WHAT KIND of reasoning our operators support. The key insight: operators aren't just transformations — they're TYPED transformations, and the type tells you what causal claims you can make.

A type system on operators could detect Rung Collapse at 'compile time' — before an AI system makes an invalid causal inference. This connects to Entry 40 (Operator Type Signatures), Entry 49 (Galois Connections as soundness guarantees), and Entry 51 (sycophancy as L2→L1 collapse under social pressure).

• Entry 22: Operators as Causal Interventions — L2 operators as interchange interventions
• Entry 38: Category Theory Semantics — the categorical framework for rung structure
• Entry 49: Galois Connections — soundness guarantees for operator abstractions
• Entry 51: Sycophancy as operator perturbation — rung collapse under social pressure
